_comment: PHASE C-2 — SHILOH / PITTSBURG LANDING, April 6-7, 1862 — the FIRST WESTERN THEATER battle for the __FIELD real-time engine (single-phase, the Fredericksburg pattern). The first major battle of the Western theater: Grant's Army of the Tennessee, encamped at Pittsburg Landing on the Tennessee River, is surprised by Johnston's Army of the Mississippi striking from Corinth, Mississippi. The first day (April 6) is a Confederate near-breakthrough — the 'Hornets' Nest' holds for hours, Johnston is killed at the Peach Orchard, and the Union is pressed into a tight perimeter around Pittsburg Landing. Overnight Buell's Army of the Ohio arrives; the second day (April 7) the reinforced Union drives the Confederates back to Corinth. The battle is modeled as a SINGLE-PHASE engagement (the first day's Confederate assault, with the second day's Union counterattack as the time-limit pressure — the defender must hold until Buell arrives). COORDINATE NOTE: the engine field is 1200x900 yards; per engine convention the ATTACKER (CS, the first day) sits at HIGH z facing -z (facing 0) and the DEFENDER (US) at LOW z facing +z (facing PI). The Confederate approach is from the southwest (high z); the Union camps and the Landing are at low z. The Hornets' Nest (the Sunken Road) is the defender's key hold line at mid-field. ARTILLERY uses the D75 UNIVERSAL GUN MODEL (real gun counts + realistic crew; fire = guns * the universal weight). ANTI-LOST-CAUSE: the lessons are command failure on BOTH sides — Grant's unprepared encampment (no entrenchments, no pickets despite knowing Johnston was at Corinth), Johnston's fatal exposure at the Peach Orchard, the piecemeal Confederate assault that failed to crush the Union before Buell arrived, and the appalling cost (~24,000 total casualties, more than the American Revolution, the War of 1812, and the Mexican-American War combined up to that point). The 'Hornets' Nest' is a monument to the cost of linear defense against massed artillery, not to Southern invincibility. Strengths are Inferred division/brigade-scale approximations (the OOBs differ across Cunningham, Sword, and the Official Records); the Verified, teaching-bearing facts are the commanders + their fate, the terrain, and the casualty weight. WEAPONS NOTE (D235): BOTH armies' infantry are tagged 'smooth' — in April 1862 both were predominantly smoothbore-armed (US mostly M1842 .69s and conversions with a minority of M1841/M1855 rifles; CS mostly obsolete smoothbores and shotguns plus a few thousand blockade-run Enfields (the Staff Ride Handbook's count is ~4,000) — U.S. Army Staff Ride Handbook for Shiloh; Cunningham). The earlier all-US-'rifled' / all-CS-'smooth' tagging overstated a clean firepower divide neither army had; the D74 model now sees the armies' real near-parity in small arms, and the Hornets' Nest collapse is driven by the accurate Ruggles grand-battery input (cs_ruggles_battery), not a false Union range advantage. Sources: NPS Shiloh National Military Park, American Battlefield Trust, the Official Records (via aotw.org), Cunningham 'Shiloh and the Western Campaign of 1862', Sword 'Shiloh: Bloody April', McDonough 'Shiloh — In Hell before Night'.

weather.note: April 6 was fair and springlike (fruit trees in blossom days before); the famous torrential cold rain came overnight April 6-7, after the day's fight — outside the modeled daytime window.
sources: Ulysses S. Grant, Personal Memoirs of U.S. Grant (1885): 'During the night rain fell in torrents' (verified verbatim — refers to the night AFTER Day 1)
William T. Sherman, letter of April 3, 1862: 'The weather is now springlike, apples and peaches in blossom' (fair pre-battle conditions)
American Battlefield Trust, 'Battle of Shiloh' battle page (battlefields.org) — live; documents the April 3 rain delay and the dawn assault

shiloh.name: Shiloh — the Hornets' Nest
shiloh.place: Pittsburg Landing, Hardin County, Tennessee — along the Tennessee River
shiloh.blurb: The first great battle of the Western theater — two days and nearly 24,000 casualties. Grant did not expect Johnston's attack, but Col. Everett Peabody's unauthorized patrol met the Confederate advance before it reached the camps, preventing total tactical surprise. The first day became a succession of Union stands, including the wider Hornets' Nest sector held chiefly by W. H. L. Wallace's veterans alongside remnants of Prentiss's command. Johnston was killed at the Peach Orchard; his loss mattered, but claims that he would certainly have destroyed Grant are counterfactual. Buell's arrival strengthened the Union counterattack on April 7 rather than single-handedly saving a doomed army.
shiloh.provenance: Verified (high) for commanders, casualties, Peabody's warning, W. H. L. Wallace's central role, and the modern corrections to the Sunken Road, Buell, and Johnston myths; Inferred (medium) for per-unit strengths and exact arrival minutes. Core C72 sources: NPS, 'Was General Grant Surprised by the Confederate Attack at Shiloh?' (https://www.nps.gov/articles/000/was-general-grant-surprised-by-the-confederate-attack-at-shiloh.htm); Timothy B. Smith, American Battlefield Trust, 'Battle of Shiloh: Shattering Myths' (https://www.battlefields.org/learn/articles/battle-shiloh-shattering-myths). Additional sources: NPS Shiloh National Military Park; Official Records; Cunningham; Sword; McDonough. Grant's surprise was a command failure, not a complete tactical ambush; Johnston's survival does not guarantee victory; the Hornets' Nest was a broad defensive sector, not a trench-like road or a Prentiss-only stand.
shiloh._homeEdgeNote: E47 (D262/D275): role-aware home edges — the Shiloh flip, re-attempted after E49a envelopment-surrender (D258), E49b straggler-shedding (D261), E53-v2 attacker parity (D273), and E54 pocket-collapse capture (D275) landed the mechanisms whose absence turned earlier attempts red. This battle seats the US defender toward LOW z (Grant's line falls back on Pittsburg Landing at z 120-180; Buell's reinforcements enter z 130-140) and the CS attacker at HIGH z (Johnston's corps waves at z 760-870 striking from Corinth), the inverse of the engine's default side-keyed edges — so before this field a routing US unit fled toward z=960 THROUGH the Confederate assault line, inflating defender casualties into a near-costless CS assault reading (the D235-panel E47 artifact and its anti-Lost-Cause teaching liability). Sherman's division, correctly seated forward at Shiloh Church (z 780), now routs toward its OWN rear. Rout, fallback, officer-ride, and supply-train direction all follow this declaration; with E54, a collapsed attacker-held pocket records broken defenders as captured before the end ledger freezes.

menu.title: SHILOH (1862)
menu.deck: The first great Western battle — command either side in the first day's fight. Lead Johnston's surprise assault and try to crush Grant before Buell arrives, or hold the Hornets' Nest and the Landing as the Union rallies.
menu.aria: Shiloh, April 1862 — the first great Western battle. Command either army in the first day's fight: the Confederate surprise assault or the Union defense at the Hornets' Nest and Pittsburg Landing.

sides.heading: Shiloh — Take Which Command?
sides.intro: April 6, 1862 · the first great battle of the Western theater. Lead the army of your choosing.

US.title: Command the Union (Grant's army)
US.deck: Hold the Sunken Road and the Landing against Johnston's surprise assault. Do what Grant did: rally the broken regiments, hold the Hornets' Nest, and buy the hours until Buell arrives.

CS.title: Command the Confederacy (Johnston's army)
CS.deck: Strike from Corinth and drive the Union into the Tennessee River before Buell's army can cross. Do what Johnston could not: crush Grant before the reinforcements arrive.

sides.foot: The other army is commanded by the AI. The first day's fight — the defender must hold until the clock runs out (press V in battle to toggle fog).

brief.attack: Strike from the woods and drive the Union back. Grant did not expect a battle here, but Peabody's patrol has warned the camps before your advance reaches them. This is your war: break the successive Union stands before exhaustion, disorganization, gunboats, and reinforcements close the opportunity.
brief.defend: Hold the modeled Hornets' Nest sector and the Landing. Peabody's patrol exposed the advance before it reached the camps, but Grant's army is unprepared and being driven back. This is your war: preserve the final line so the reinforced army can counterattack.

objective.name: The Hornets' Nest Road to Pittsburg Landing
objective.note: The wider Hornets' Nest corridor covering the road back to Pittsburg Landing. The single wall-like hold line is a game abstraction, not a claim that the farm road was deeply sunken or supplied trench-like cover. If the Confederate attacker clears this modeled sector, Grant falls back toward the Landing; if the Union holds, the final line survives and reinforcements strengthen the next day's counterattack.

leaders._note: fate = a per-leader durability multiplier weighting the casualty model to HISTORY (>1 endures, <1 is fall-prone). At Shiloh, Albert Sidney Johnston was killed (the highest-ranking general on either side to die in the war); Wallace (US) was mortally wounded; Prentiss was captured; the others survived. The Confederate command was decapitated at the worst moment — Johnston's death at ~2:30 p.m. on April 6 cost the assault its driving hand.

US.name: Maj. Gen. Ulysses S. Grant
US.note: Commanded the Army of the Tennessee. Encamped without entrenchments at Pittsburg Landing despite knowing Johnston was at Corinth — a major command failure. Spent the first day rallying broken regiments and forming the last line. His composure under fire saved the army. He survived. (Verified)
US.teach: Gen. Grant steadies the last line at the Landing — the general who was surprised now holds the army together by sheer composure.

US.name: Brig. Gen. Benjamin M. Prentiss
US.note: Rallied a remnant of his shattered division in the Hornets' Nest sector alongside the veteran brigades of W. H. L. Wallace's division. Surrounded and captured late on April 6. Prentiss later became the best-known narrator of the defense, but modern scholarship rejects the Prentiss-only version. (Verified)
US.teach: Gen. Prentiss and his remnant hold beside W. H. L. Wallace's veterans before the position is surrounded.

US.name: Brig. Gen. W. H. L. Wallace
US.note: Commanded the 2nd Division, Army of the Tennessee. Mortally wounded at the Hornets' Nest as his division was overrun; struck April 6 and died April 10, 1862, at the Cherry Mansion in Savannah, Tennessee. His loss was a severe blow to the Union command. (Verified - death date corrected: April 10, not May 10, 1862.)
US.teach: Gen. Wallace is mortally struck at the Hornets' Nest — the division commander falls with his men.

US.name: Brig. Gen. William T. Sherman
US.note: Commanded the 5th Division, posted at Shiloh Church on the Union right. His division bore the first shock of the Confederate assault; he was wounded twice (hand and shoulder) but stayed in command. His steadiness under fire — after being called insane — was the making of his reputation. He survived. (Verified)
US.teach: Gen. Sherman is hit twice and keeps fighting — the man called 'insane' proves his mettle in the worst hour.

CS.name: Gen. Albert Sidney Johnston
CS.note: Commanded the Army of the Mississippi and was the Confederacy's highest-ranking field commander. Killed at ~2:30 p.m. near the Peach Orchard after a bullet severed an artery. His loss disrupted command, but the claim that his survival would certainly have destroyed Grant is an unprovable Lost Cause counterfactual. (Verified death; counterfactual rejected)
CS.teach: Gen. Albert Sidney Johnston is struck behind the knee and bleeds out in minutes — the Confederate commander falls at the moment of his greatest opportunity.

CS.name: Maj. Gen. Braxton Bragg
CS.note: Commanded the II Corps. After Johnston's death, Beauregard assumed overall command; Bragg directed the center of the assault line, where he personally ordered the repeated frontal assaults on the Hornets' Nest for some five hours. His piecemeal commitment of reserves — feeding brigades in one at a time instead of massing — was a major reason the Confederate assault failed to crush the Union before nightfall. He survived. (Verified - sector corrected: Johnston attacked in successive corps lines, not formal wings; Bragg's Hornets' Nest assaults fell on the center - Duncan Field / Sunken Road - matching the teaching card.)
CS.teach: Gen. Bragg feeds his brigades in piecemeal — the assault that might have won is blunted by disjointed command.

CS.name: Maj. Gen. William J. Hardee
CS.note: Commanded the III Corps. His corps opened the battle, striking Sherman's division at Shiloh Church. Hardee was one of the Confederacy's most capable corps commanders; he survived. (Verified)
CS.teach: Gen. Hardee's corps drives toward Shiloh Church after Peabody's patrol has exposed the advance — Grant is surprised, but the camps are not struck without warning.

CS.name: Brig. Gen. John C. Breckinridge
CS.note: Commanded the Reserve Corps. Former U.S. Vice President (under Buchanan). His reserve was committed late and disjointedly; he survived to become Confederate Secretary of War in 1865. (Verified - rank corrected: he was a BRIGADIER general at Shiloh; his major-generalcy is dated April 14, 1862, eight days AFTER the battle - earned partly for his Shiloh performance.)
CS.teach: Gen. Breckinridge's reserve arrives — but the day's best chance has already passed.

US.name: Prentiss's Division (the Hornets' Nest)
US.note: 6th Division, Army of the Tennessee. Most of the division was scattered early; its remnant joined W. H. L. Wallace's veterans in the wider Hornets' Nest sector. Prentiss was captured late in the day. The farm road was not a trench-like sunken defense. (Verified identity/context; Inferred strength)

US.name: Sherman's Division (Shiloh Church)
US.note: 5th Division, posted on the Union right at Shiloh Church. Bore the first shock of the Confederate assault; driven back in confusion but rallied. Sherman was wounded twice and stayed in command. (Verified identity; Inferred strength)

US.name: McClernand's Division
US.note: 1st Division, Army of the Tennessee. Held the center near the Hamburg-Purdy Road; fought a stubborn delaying action through the morning. (Verified identity; Inferred strength)

US.name: Hurlbut's Division
US.note: 4th Division, Army of the Tennessee. Held the left center near the Peach Orchard; fought a stubborn defense before being driven back toward the Landing. (Verified identity; Inferred strength)

US.name: W. H. L. Wallace's Division
US.commander: Brig. Gen. W. H. L. Wallace
US.note: 2nd Division, Army of the Tennessee. Its veteran brigades formed the principal strength of the Hornets' Nest line, joined by remnants of Prentiss's command. Wallace was mortally wounded as the position collapsed. (Verified identity/context; Inferred strength)

US.name: Battery A, 1st Illinois Light Artillery
US.note: Battery A (McClernand's division) in the Union line covering the Hornets' Nest sector. Universal gun model (D75): 6 guns, ~94 crew. (Verified - commander corrected: the prior 'Capt. Ezra Taylor' commanded Battery B / was Sherman's division chief of artillery; Battery A was Capt. Peter P. Wood's.)

US.name: Battery B, 1st Illinois Light Artillery
US.note: Massed with other batteries near the Hornets' Nest to create the 'artillery hell' that broke the Confederate assaults. Universal gun model (D75): 6 guns, ~94 crew. (Verified)

CS.name: Hardee's Corps (the first wave)
CS.note: III Corps, Army of the Mississippi. Opened the main assault toward Sherman's division after Peabody's patrol had met the advance. Grant's command was surprised, but Union units formed before the camps were struck. (Verified identity/context; Inferred strength)

CS.name: Bragg's Corps (the second wave)
CS.note: II Corps, Army of the Mississippi. The second wave, intended to exploit Hardee's breakthrough. Bragg fed his brigades in piecemeal — a major command failure that cost the Confederacy the battle. (Verified identity; Inferred strength)

CS.name: Polk's Corps
CS.note: I Corps, Army of the Mississippi. An Episcopal bishop turned general; his corps fought in the center and left. (Verified identity; Inferred strength)

CS.name: Breckinridge's Reserve Corps
CS.note: Reserve Corps. Committed late and disjointedly after Johnston's death — the reserve that might have crushed the Union was never massed. (Verified identity; Inferred strength. Rank: BRIG. GEN. at the battle - MG dated April 14, 1862.)

CS.name: Confederate Artillery (massed batteries)
CS.note: Massed Confederate batteries (Cobb's Kentucky Battery, Byrne's, Robertson's, etc.) supporting the assault. Universal gun model (D75): 12 guns, ~390 crew — the DISTRIBUTED morning close-support batteries; the ~4 p.m. massing of the grand battery arrives separately as the timed cs_ruggles_battery reinforcement. (Verified - commander corrected: the prior 'Col. John J. Trabue' was Col. Robert P. Trabue, who led the Kentucky 'Orphan' INFANTRY brigade in Breckinridge's reserve, NOT the artillery.)

reinforcements.name: Buell's Army — Ammen's Brigade (the first across)
reinforcements.entry: from the steamboats at Pittsburg Landing — the first of Buell's Army of the Ohio
reinforcements.note: The leading brigade of Buell's Army of the Ohio, crossing the Tennessee River on steamboats through the night of April 6-7. Ammen's men were the first reinforcements to reach Grant's beleaguered line. (Verified - rank corrected: Ammen was a COLONEL (24th Ohio) at Shiloh, commanding the 10th Brigade of Nelson's division by seniority; his brigadier-generalcy dated July 16, 1862, after the battle.)

reinforcements.name: Buell's Army — Nelson's Division
reinforcements.entry: Buell's division arriving at the Landing
reinforcements.note: Nelson's 4th Division, Army of the Ohio. Nelson was a massive, profane, energetic commander who personally rallied broken Union regiments on the evening of April 6. His division formed the core of the Union counterattack on April 7. (Verified)

reinforcements.name: Ruggles's Grand Battery (53 guns)
reinforcements.entry: massing wheel to wheel opposite the Hornets' Nest — the largest concentration of guns yet seen on a North American field
reinforcements.note: Brig. Gen. Daniel Ruggles's grand battery: ~53-62 guns assembled from batteries across the army at ~4:00-4:30 p.m. and massed ~400 yards from the Sunken Road. Its bombardment — together with the infantry encirclement of the collapsing flanks and empty cartridge boxes — broke the position at last (the NPS narrative gives the guns causal primacy; recent scholarship, e.g. Timothy B. Smith, weighs the encirclement more heavily — both agree the massing happened at this scale). The accurate documented input in the Hornets' Nest's collapse (D92/D74 — no output fudge). Universal gun model (D75): 53 guns (the conservative low bound of the 53-62 documented), ~1,200 crew. The game clock is compressed and non-linear (this file's dusk Chalmers assault enters at atSec 120): atSec 100 stands in for the ~4 p.m. massing, keeping the real event order — the grand battery masses BEFORE the final dusk assaults. (Verified event/scale: NPS Shiloh 'Ruggles' Battery' tour stop; American Battlefield Trust; Inferred crew/timing mapping)

reinforcements.name: Chalmers's Brigade (the final assault)
reinforcements.entry: the last evening assault, pressing toward Pittsburg Landing
reinforcements.note: Chalmers's Mississippi brigade made one of the final Confederate attacks at dusk on April 6, driving toward Pittsburg Landing — checked by Webster's massed Union guns and the river gunboats. It represents the late, piecemeal evening assaults that could not break the line before Buell crossed. (Verified - the prior 'Wood's Reserve Brigade / last uncommitted reserve' was a misattribution: S. A. M. Wood led a FRONT-wave brigade in Hardee's Corps that opened the battle, not a reserve.)

hills.name: the Pittsburg Landing ridge
hills.note: The high ground above the Tennessee River landing — the Union's last defensive line on April 6. (Verified)

hills.name: the high ground at Shiloh Church
hills.note: The ridge around the small Methodist meetinghouse (Shiloh Church) where Sherman's division was posted and first struck. (Verified)

hills.name: the Peach Orchard ridge
hills.note: The low ridge where A.S. Johnston was killed at ~2:30 p.m. on April 6. (Verified)

woods.note: the tangled woodland of the wider Hornets' Nest sector. The wartime name referred to the intensity of fire; the adjacent farm road was not a trench-like sunken defensive work. (Verified context; exact geometry Inferred)
the woods on the Confederate right — the approach route of Hardee's and Bragg's corps. (Verified)
the woods on the Confederate left — the approach route of Polk's corps. (Verified)
the timber along the River Road — the Union reserve and rallying ground. (Verified)

walls.note: the HORNETS' NEST SECTOR — GAME ABSTRACTION: this wall-shaped gameplay line represents a wider, irregular defensive sector. The real farm road was not deeply sunken and did not provide trench-like cover. The geometry is retained for playability, not presented as verified terrain. (Verified correction; Inferred gameplay mapping)

markers.name: the Corinth Road
markers.note: The main road from Corinth to Pittsburg Landing — the Confederate axis of advance. (Verified)

markers.name: the Hamburg-Purdy Road
markers.note: The road running east-west across the battlefield, intersecting the Corinth Road near Shiloh Church. (Verified)

markers.name: the River Road
markers.note: The road along the Tennessee River — the Union line of retreat and Buell's approach route. (Verified)

markers.name: Shiloh Church
markers.note: The small Methodist meetinghouse that gave the battle its name — Sherman's division was posted here and struck first.

markers.name: the Peach Orchard
markers.note: Where A.S. Johnston was killed at ~2:30 p.m. — the Confederate commander falls at the moment of his greatest opportunity.

markers.name: the Hornets' Nest (modeled sector)
markers.note: A gameplay abstraction for the wider sector held chiefly by W. H. L. Wallace's veterans alongside remnants of Prentiss's command; the road was not a trench-like defense.

markers.name: Pittsburg Landing
markers.note: The Tennessee River landing — the Union's last line and Buell's arrival point.

markers.name: Lick Creek
markers.note: The creek on the Union left that channeled the Confederate approach and limited the frontage of the assault. (Verified)

markers.name: Owl Creek
markers.note: The creek on the Union right that limited the Confederate flanking options. (Verified)

US.US: History is overturned. Your army held the modeled Hornets' Nest sector and the Landing rather than falling back through successive positions. Historically Grant's surprised but warned army formed a strong final line before Buell's arrival strengthened the next day's counterattack.
US.CS: History is overturned. The Union held the Sunken Road and the Landing — the ground your army historically carried in the first day's assault. In April 1862 Johnston's surprise attack nearly swept Grant into the river; under your defense the Confederate assault broke against the Hornets' Nest and never reached the Landing.

CS.US: History holds. As on April 6, the unexpected Confederate assault drove the Union through successive stands, surrounded the Hornets' Nest defenders, and compressed Grant's army around Pittsburg Landing. Peabody's warning prevented a total camp ambush, and Grant's final line stood before Buell strengthened the counterattack.
CS.CS: History holds. As in April 1862, your surprise assault carried the field — the Union camps were overrun, the Hornets' Nest was surrounded, and Grant's army was pressed into a tight perimeter around Pittsburg Landing. But Johnston's death at the Peach Orchard cost the assault its driving hand, and the piecemeal attacks that followed failed to crush the Union before Buell's army crossed the river.

endNote.draw: A bloodier stalemate than history's clear Confederate first-day victory — but the Hornets' Nest held long enough that the battle's outcome was still undecided when the light failed.

teaching.title: Shiloh — the War Learns Its Cost

cards.head: The cost that shocked the nation
cards.body: About 24,000 men were killed, wounded, or missing at Shiloh — more than the American Revolution, the War of 1812, and the Mexican-American War combined. The nation was stunned: no one had imagined that a single battle could produce such slaughter. Grant later wrote that Shiloh 'gave the death-blow to the idea that the war would be a short one.' The age of industrial war had arrived, and Shiloh was its first American lesson.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) — American Battlefield Trust (13,047 US / 10,699 CS / 23,746 total); Grant's Memoirs.

cards.head: Surprised — but warned before the camps were struck
cards.body: Grant did not expect Johnston to attack at Pittsburg Landing; his army was unentrenched and organized for an offensive. That command surprise was real. Total tactical surprise is different: Col. Everett Peabody sent an unauthorized patrol before dawn, met Confederate skirmishers in Fraley Field, and gave Union units time to form before the main advance reached their camps. Peabody prevented a camp ambush, while Grant and Sherman had discounted mounting evidence that a major attack was near.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) — NPS, 'Was General Grant Surprised by the Confederate Attack at Shiloh?' (https://www.nps.gov/articles/000/was-general-grant-surprised-by-the-confederate-attack-at-shiloh.htm); Timothy B. Smith, American Battlefield Trust, 'Battle of Shiloh: Shattering Myths' (https://www.battlefields.org/learn/articles/battle-shiloh-shattering-myths).

cards.head: Johnston's death — consequence without certainty
cards.body: Albert Sidney Johnston, the Confederacy's highest-ranking field commander, was struck behind the knee near the Peach Orchard and bled to death after the bullet severed an artery. Losing the army commander mattered and complicated an already disordered assault. It does not follow that Johnston would certainly have destroyed Grant had he lived: Grant's artillery-backed final line, ravines, gunboats, Confederate exhaustion and disorganization, and arriving Union formations still shaped the day. The familiar might-have-won story is a counterfactual, not a verified outcome.
cards.provenance: Verified death; counterfactual disputed — NPS Shiloh; Timothy B. Smith, American Battlefield Trust, 'Battle of Shiloh: Shattering Myths' (https://www.battlefields.org/learn/articles/battle-shiloh-shattering-myths); Official Records.

cards.head: The piecemeal assault that lost a battle
cards.body: The Confederate advance lost alignment as corps, divisions, and brigades became entangled, and repeated attacks struck different parts of the Union line without decisive coordination. The Hornets' Nest eventually fell after its flanks collapsed, the defenders were surrounded, and Ruggles massed artillery against the sector. Those failures explain why the first day's gains stopped short of destroying Grant's army; they do not prove that one commander or one different order guaranteed victory. The lesson is the cost of disjointed command, not a Lost Cause tale of inevitable victory narrowly missed.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) — Sword, 'Shiloh: Bloody April'; McDonough, 'Shiloh — In Hell before Night'; Cunningham.

cards.head: The Hornets' Nest — sector, soldiers, and later memory
cards.body: The Hornets' Nest was a wider defensive sector, not a trench formed by a deeply sunken road. W. H. L. Wallace's veteran brigades supplied most of its strength, joined by the remnant of Prentiss's division and other Union troops. The position resisted repeated attacks before Confederate gains on both flanks surrounded it; concentrated artillery contributed to the collapse, but encirclement was decisive. Prentiss later became the defense's dominant public narrator, helping turn a shared stand into a Prentiss-centered legend. In this game the single wall-like objective is an explicit GAME ABSTRACTION for that wider, irregular sector.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) — Timothy B. Smith, American Battlefield Trust, 'Battle of Shiloh: Shattering Myths' (https://www.battlefields.org/learn/articles/battle-shiloh-shattering-myths); NPS, 'Was General Grant Surprised by the Confederate Attack at Shiloh?' (https://www.nps.gov/articles/000/was-general-grant-surprised-by-the-confederate-attack-at-shiloh.htm); NPS Shiloh battlefield interpretation.

codex.title: Shiloh / Pittsburg Landing, April 6-7, 1862
codex.summary: The first great battle of the Western theater. Grant did not expect Johnston's attack, but Peabody's unauthorized patrol warned the army before the camps were struck. On April 6 the Confederates drove the Union through successive stands; W. H. L. Wallace's veterans and remnants of Prentiss's command fought in the wider Hornets' Nest sector before being surrounded. Johnston's death disrupted Confederate command without making any lost victory certain. Grant formed a strong final line, and Buell's arrival strengthened the April 7 counterattack. Nearly 24,000 casualties taught both sides that the war would be long and terrible.
